coredata.dc:title: Bioinspired carbon quantum dots nanofluid fabricated by a facile two-step synthesis toward high performance lubrication
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Hongxiang
Zuo, Xiangyu
Zhang, Xia
Wang, Xiaobo
Zhou, Feng
coredata.dc:description: High-efficiency lubrication required by modern advanced industry cannot be met by single solid lubricant or liquid lubricant. The nanofluid combining the advantages of solid and liquid lubrication has become a very promising lubricating material. Here, inspired by the lubrication mechanism of snail mucus, a carbon quantum dots nanofluid is fabricated by a facile two-step synthesis. Firstly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared by citric acid (CA) pyrolysis. Then, CQDs rich in carboxyl groups on the surface are used as crosslinking sites for polyethyleneimine, preparing high-performance nanofluid lubricants with three-dimensional and multiple adsorption networks. The nanofluids perform good fluidity under room temperature and excellent tribological behavior. The nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA shows a low friction coefficient of 0.091, and the wear decreases by 52% compared to PAO-4. This three-dimensional network structure endows the nanofluid with good load-bearing capacity, forming a tough lubricating film at the friction interface. The multiple adsorption from -NH2, -COOH, and -CONH- in nanofluids can effectively enhance the bonding force between the nanofluid and substrate, forming a stable lubricating film. Furthermore, CQDs can act as nanobearings, converting sliding friction into rolling friction. Additionally, the nanofluid can also serve as the water-based lubricant additive with excellent performance, thereinto the 0.5 wt% addition of nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) reduces friction by 52% and wear by 41% compared to pure water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofluid
Carbon quantum dots
Liquid lubrication
Lubricant additive
Lubricating film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Snail mucus structure and synthesis strategy for carbon quantum dots nanofluid.

Photographs, fluorescence characteristics, and internal components of carbon quantum dots nanofluid. (a) Photographs of nanofluids containing CQD/CA of different concentrations (2.5 wt%, 5 wt%,7.5 wt%,10 wt%) before and after reversing the glass vials. (b) Fluorescence characteristics of CQD/CA and nanofluids dispersed in deionized water (ultraviolet wavelength = 365 nm). (c) TEM image, (d) HRTEM image, (c1) size distribution, and (d1, d2) lattice fringe spacing of CQD/CA. (e) HRTEM image and (e1) lattice fringe spacing of nanofluid containing 5 wt% CQD/CA.

Physical and chemical properties of CQD/CA and nanofluids. (a) FT-IR spectra of the CQD/CA, PEI, and nanofluid containing 5 wt% CQD/CA. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of nanofluids containing CQD/CA of different concentrations. (c) TGA curves of nanofluids. (d) XPS full spectra of CQD/CA and nanofluid containing 5 wt% CQD/CA. High-resolution XPS spectra of CQD/CA: (e) C 1 s and (f) O 1 s. High-resolution XPS spectra of nanofluid containing 5 wt% CQD/CA: (g) C 1 s, (h) O 1 s, and (i) N 1 s.

Rheological behavior investigation of nanofluids containing CQD/CA of different concentrations. (a) Shear stress variations versus shear rate. (b) Viscosity variations versus shear rate. (c) Viscosity (Shear rate = 100 1/s) comparison of nanofluids. (d) Storage modulus and loss modulus versus temperature. (e) Shear stress variations versus temperature. (f) Viscosity variations versus temperature. (g) Schematic diagram of rheological test.

Measurement of the tribological performance of nanofluids. (a) The friction coefficient curves, (b) average friction coefficients, and (c) wear volumes of PAO-4 and nanofluids containing CQD/CA of different concentrations (Friction conditions: Load = 100 N, frequency = 2 Hz, amplitude = 2 mm, time = 30 min). (d) The friction coefficient curves, (e) average friction coefficients, and (f) wear volumes of nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA under different loads (Friction conditions: Load = 100 N, 200 N, 300 N, and 400 N, frequency = 2 Hz, amplitude = 2 mm, time = 30 min). (g) The friction coefficient curves, (h) average friction coefficients, and (i) wear volumes of nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA under different frequencies (Friction conditions: Load = 100 N, frequency = 2 Hz, 4 Hz, 6 Hz, and 8 Hz, amplitude = 2 mm, time = 30 min).

Characterizations of wear scars formed by PAO-4 and nanofluids containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA. (a1, b1) The depths, (a2, b2) three-dimensional images, (a3, b3) widths, (a4, b4) surface morphologies, (a5, b5) elemental distribution of wear scars formed by PAO-4 and nanofluids containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA.

(a) Images and (b) comparison of contact angles on steel surface of pure water and nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) with various concentrations (0.05 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%) dispersed in water. (c) Potentiodynamic polarization curves, (d) corrosion current, and corrosion potential of 10 wt% NaCl aqueous solution and 0.5 wt% nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) (10 wt% NaCl). (e) Images of polished coppers after being immersed in pure water without NaCl and pure water containing 0.5 wt% nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) for 8 h at 55 °C. (f) The friction coefficient curves, (g) average friction coefficients, and (h) wear volumes of pure water and nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) with various concentrations (0.05 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%) dispersed in water. Friction condition: Load = 30 N, frequency = 3 Hz, amplitude = 2 mm, time = 30 min.

Characterizations of wear scars formed by pure water and 0.5 wt% nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) dispersed in water. (a1, b1) The depths, (a2, b2) three-dimensional images, (a3, b3) widths, (a4, b4) surface morphologies, (a5, b5) elemental distribution of wear scars formed by pure water and nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) with various concentrations dispersed in water.

High-resolution XPS spectra of pure water: (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, and (c) Fe 2p. High-resolution XPS spectra of pure water added with 0.5 wt% nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA): (d) C 1 s, (e) O 1 s, (f) Fe 2p, and (g) N 1 s. (h) Schematic diagram of lubrication mechanism.
Lubrication mechanism diagram of carbon quantum dots nanofluid.

head.ce:title: Bioinspired carbon quantum dots nanofluid fabricated by a facile two-step synthesis toward high performance lubrication

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by the lubrication mechanism of snail mucus, a carbon quantum dots nanofluid is fabricated by a facile two-step synthesis. The nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA shows a low friction coefficient of 0.091, and the wear decreases by 52% compared to PAO-4. Additionally, the nanofluid can also serve as the water-based lubricant additive with excellent performance, thereinto the 0.5 wt% addition of nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) reduces friction by 52% and wear by 41% compared to pure water.

High-efficiency lubrication required by modern advanced industry cannot be met by single solid lubricant or liquid lubricant. The nanofluid combining the advantages of solid and liquid lubrication has become a very promising lubricating material. Here, inspired by the lubrication mechanism of snail mucus, a carbon quantum dots nanofluid is fabricated by a facile two-step synthesis. Firstly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared by citric acid (CA) pyrolysis. Then, CQDs rich in carboxyl groups on the surface are used as crosslinking sites for polyethyleneimine, preparing high-performance nanofluid lubricants with three-dimensional and multiple adsorption networks. The nanofluids perform good fluidity under room temperature and excellent tribological behavior. The nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA shows a low friction coefficient of 0.091, and the wear decreases by 52% compared to PAO-4. This three-dimensional network structure endows the nanofluid with good load-bearing capacity, forming a tough lubricating film at the friction interface. The multiple adsorption from -NH2, -COOH, and -CONH- in nanofluids can effectively enhance the bonding force between the nanofluid and substrate, forming a stable lubricating film. Furthermore, CQDs can act as nanobearings, converting sliding friction into rolling friction. Additionally, the nanofluid can also serve as the water-based lubricant additive with excellent performance, thereinto the 0.5 wt% addition of nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) reduces friction by 52% and wear by 41% compared to pure water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear, as a common phenomenon in the operation of mechanical moving parts, can accelerate mechanical damage and shorten machine life, causing huge energy waste and economic losses [1–5]. According to incomplete statistics, friction consumes about 1/3 of the global primary energy, and wear causes about 60 % of equipment failures or malfunctions. Mechanical equipment accidents caused by friction and wear problems account for over 50 % [6–10]. Reducing friction and wear has always been an important issue for workers in the field of tribology, as well as the key in the development of precision machining, intelligent manufacturing, and green environmental protection [11–15]. Therefore, researchers are working hard to develop lubricating materials with excellent anti-wear and friction reducing properties [16–20]. Among numerous lubricating materials, carbon materials have attracted widespread attention from researchers due to their excellent lubricating properties [21]. Thereinto, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials have become a research hotspot[22–28]. CQDs are ultrafine, dispersed, torispherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm. In addition, CQDs can be prepared by various methods, including arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis, chemical oxidation, combustion, hydrothermal synthesis, microwave, template method, etc [29–33]. Meanwhile, CQDs have many advantages such as good water solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, wide source of raw materials, low cost, and good biocompatibility. Moreover, CQDs show promising application prospects in various fields, such as medical imaging technology [34], environmental monitoring, chemical analysis, catalyst preparation [35], energy development, etc [36,37]. In the field of lubrication, CQDs also display many advantages. Firstly, compared to traditional carbon nanomaterials, the small and uniform size of CQDs provides good dispersion stability in base lubricants [38]. In addition, there are abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, which facilitate their adsorption on the friction interface and exert the lubricating effect [39]. Furthermore, CQDs can act as nanobearing in sliding contact, converting sliding friction into rolling friction [40]. Therefore, CQDs are ideal lubricating materials.

Snails in nature rely on secreted mucus to ensure stable crawling (Fig. 1 ). The collagen, glycoproteins, and D-Glucuronic acid in snail mucus provide multiple adsorption effects, which improve the adhesion between mucus and contact surfaces [41–43], thereby endowing snail mucus with excellent lubrication ability on various surfaces. In addition, snail mucus possesses a three-dimensional gel network and liquid like fluidity, which can form a continuous lubricating film on the contact surface to assist crawling [43]. This indicates that lubricants with three-dimensional and multiple adsorption networks can form stable and robust lubricating films at the friction interface, thereby exhibiting excellent lubrication properties. The lubrication mechanism of snail mucus provides inspiration for developing high-performance lubricants. At present, although some biomimetic lubricants can reduce friction, the preparation process is complex [43]. On the other hand, the intricate preparation process increases the production cost of lubricants, limiting their large-scale application. Therefore, developing a biomimetic lubricant with simple preparation, low cost, and high performance remains a challenge. In addition, nanofluid is a special suspension formed by adding nanoscale metal or non-metal particles to the base liquid. Combining the advantages of the base liquid and nanoparticles, nanofluid has excellent heat transfer and tribological properties. Developing nanofluid lubricants with excellent anti-wear and friction reducing properties has become a research hotspot.

In this work, we demonstrate a novel and facile two-step synthesis strategy for preparing high-performance nanofluid lubricants with three-dimensional and multiple adsorption networks using carbon quantum dots as crosslinking sites between polyethyleneimine chains, overcoming complex preparation processes and high costs. Firstly, the fluidity, internal composition, physicochemical properties, and rheological behavior of nanofluids were investigated. Tribological tests have shown that nanofluids possess excellent tribological properties, with the wear of the nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) decreased by 52 % compared to PAO-4. Subsequently, the effects of load and frequency on the tribological properties of nanofluids are further investigated. Meanwhile, the nanofluid can also serve as the water-based lubricant additive with excellent performance, thereinto the 0.5 wt% addition of nanofluid exhibits the best anti-wear and friction reducing performance, reducing friction by 52 % and wear by 41 % compared to pure water. Compared with other carbon dot-based lubricants in reported works (Section 1.2 in supporting information), the prepared nanofluid exhibits relatively good tribological properties. The addition of nanofluids can reduce the surface tension of lubricants and improve their anti-corrosion performance. Finally, the lubrication mechanism of nanofluids is revealed through the analysis of the morphology and surface element composition of the wear scars. This study has developed a novel method for preparing nanofluids, providing new design ideas for biomimetic lubricants and promoting the development of lubrication technology.

The citric acid (CA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI, M.W. = 1800) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was provided by the Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China). The ethanol and petroleum ether were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China).

The CQDs are prepared by thermal decomposition of CA [44]. The specific method is as follows. The 20 g citric acid powder is placed in a beaker and thermal decomposition is conducted in an oil bath at 200 ℃ for 30 min. The orange yellow liquid containing carbon quantum dots is obtained. The orange yellow liquid contains a small amount of unreacted citric acid, thus the orange yellow liquid is named CQD/CA. The photograph of CQD/CA is shown in Fig. S1.

The 9 g PEI is dissolved in 30 ml deionized water placed in a 250 ml flask. The 1 g CQD/CA is weighed and dissolved in 100 ml deionized water. The obtained liquid is added dropwise into the flask under stirring conditions to thoroughly mix the two liquids mentioned above. The reaction is carried out for 24 h at 60 ℃under the reflux condition. After the reaction is complete, the obtained product is subjected to rotary evaporation for 2 h and vacuum drying at 70 ℃ for 24 h. The obtained brown liquid is the nanofluid (10 wt% CQD/CA). The nanofluids containing CQD/CA of different concentrations (2.5 wt%, 5 wt%, 7.5 wt%) are prepared by changing the ratio of polyethyleneimine to CQD/CA. The density of the nanofluids is shown in Table S1.

The universal mechanical tester (UMT) friction testing machine equipped with ball (The diameter = 8 mm) and disk, is used to investigate the tribological properties of lubricants under reciprocating mode. The alcohol and petroleum ether are utilized to clean separately the surface of steel/steel friction pair before the experiment. The lubricant is placed at the contact interface before the friction experiment. Each experiment is conducted three times to ensure accuracy.

Fluorescence images of CQD/CA and nanofluids dispersed in deionized water are obtained under ultraviolet excitation (ultraviolet wavelength = 365 nm). The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-F200, JEOL) is applied to investigate the particle size and morphology of carbon quantum dots, as well as the morphology and composition of nanofluids. The molecular structure of CQD/CA, PEI, and nanofluids is studied by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of nanofluids are acquired by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (HITACHI, U-3900H). Thermal stability of nanofluids is investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, STA449F3, Natzsch, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI5000, Thermo Fisher Scientific) is used to analyze the elemental composition and valence states of CQD/CA and nanofluids. The rheological behavior of nanofluids is studied by a rheometer (MCR302, Anton Paar, Austria). The three-dimensional (3D) profilometer (MicroXAM, KLA-Tencor) is applied to obtain the wear volume, cross-sectional curve, and 3D image of the wear scar. The morphology and wear mechanism of the wear marks are investigated using an optical microscope (XDS-0745D, China). The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, Quanta 650 FEG, USA) is used to investigate the surface element composition and distribution of wear marks. The contact angle of the lubricant on the steel surface is obtained using a contact angle measuring instrument (DSA100, Krüss). The anti-corrosion performance of lubricants is studied by an electrochemical workstation (PARSTAT 4000A). The elemental composition and valence states of the worn surface are studied using XPS.

A facile two-step synthesis strategy of carbon quantum dots nanofluid is illustrated in Fig. 1. Firstly, carbon quantum dots are prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid. Subsequently, the -COOH in carbon quantum dot and unreacted citric acid reacts chemically with - NH2 in polyethyleneimine to produce -CONH-, and the nanofluid is successfully obtained. The formation of -CONH- enables the interconnection of different polyethyleneimine chains, thereby giving the nanofluid a three-dimensional network structure. Meanwhile, the -COOH, -NH2, and -CONH- can provide multiple adsorption and enhance the adhesion of nanofluids on the substrate surface. Fig. 2 a shows photographs of nanofluids containing CQD/CA of different concentrations (2.5 wt%, 5 wt%, 7.5 wt%, 10 wt%) before and after reversing the glass vials. It can be found that all four kinds of nanofluids are transparent and brown liquids, and possess good fluidity at room temperature. The CQD/CA and nanofluids dispersed in pure water exhibit bright blue fluorescence under UV light (ultraviolet wavelength = 365 nm), which proves the successful preparation of CQDs and existence of CQDs in nanofluids (Fig. 2b). It can be observed from TEM images of CQD/CA (Fig. 2c, c1) that CQDs are uniformly distributed, with average particle diameter of 2.8 ± 0.4 nm. In addition, lattice fringe spacing of 0.193 nm and 0.210 nm attributed to CQDs can be found from HRTEM image of CQD/CA (Fig. 2d, d1, d2). The lattice fringe spacing of 0.194 nm (Fig. 2e1) can be observed from Fig. 2e, which proves the existence of CQDs in nanofluids. But CQDs in nanofluids display more concentrated distribution, which is because that separate CQDs are linked by PEI chains after chemical reaction between CQDs and PEI.

The FT-IR was utilized to study the chemical structure of nanofluid (Fig. 3 a and Fig. S2). The observed absorption peaks of CQD/CA at 3463 cm−1, 1774 cm−1, 1719 cm−1, and 1220 cm−1 corresponded to the O-H bond, C=O bond (monomer), C=O bond (dimer), and C-O bond, respectively (Fig. 3a). The signals of PEI and nanofluid at 3508 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1origin from N-H stretching vibration and N-H bending vibration, respectively. The peak of nanofluid at 1656 cm−1 is attributed to C=O bond. The FT-IR results verify the chemical reaction between -NH2 of PEI and -COOH of CQD/CA and formation of -CONH-. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of nanofluid and CQD/CA are shown in the Fig. 3b and Fig. S3. It can be observed that the CQD/CA shows ultraviolet absorption for 200–300 nm and 300–400 nm, and nanofluid exhibits significant ultraviolet absorption in the range of 200–400 nm, which proves the existence of carbon quantum dots in the nanofluid. The TGA curves of nanofluids are displayed in Fig. 3c. The TGA curves of nanofluids with various CQD/CA contents are similar and can be divided into two stages, including 30–200 ℃ and 200–500 ℃. This indicates that the content of CQD/CA has little effect on the thermal stability of nanofluids. XPS analysis was used to further the element composition and valence state of CQD/CA and nanofluid. The XPS full spectra reveal the main elements of CQD/CA (C, O) and nanofluid (C, O, and N). The detailed XPS spectra are displayed in Fig. 3e-i. The peaks of CQD/CA for C 1 s at 288.9 eV, 286.6 eV, and 284.8 eV are attributed to COOH, C-OH, and C-C bonds, respectively. The bonding energy of CQD/CA for O1s at 533.3 eV, 532.6 eV, and 531.9 eV corresponds to the C=O, C-O, and O–H bonds, respectively [6]. Peaks of nanofluid for C 1 s at 285.3 eV and 284.8 eV relate to C-N bond, C-C bond [21]. The bonding energy of nanofluid for O 1 s at 532.2 eV and 530.3 eV are attributed to C=O and O-H bonds, respectively [10]. The signal of nanofluid for N 1 s at 398.3 eV relate to C-N bond. The above XPS results also verify formation of -CONH-.

Rheological performances of nanofluids containing CQD/CA of different concentrations are illustrated in Fig. 4 . In addition, the PEI serves as a control. The device diagram for rheological test is shown in the Fig. 4g. The shear stress of nanofluids and PEI increases linearly over shear rate (Fig. 4a), indicating the nature of newtonian fluid. It can be observed from Fig. 4b that the viscosities of nanofluids and PEI keep stable under increasing shear rates. The viscosity gradually increases with the increase of CQD/CA concentration (Fig. 4c). This is because the -COOH in CA and CQD structure can react chemically with -NH2 in PEI. The CA and CQD can act as bridging sites, connecting different PEI chains to construct a three-dimensional network structure, and thereby increasing the viscosity of the nanofluid (Fig. 4g). The modulus variations versus temperature were investigated to confirm the liquid-like behavior of carbon quantum dots nanofluids (Fig. 4d). It can be found that the loss modulus G” of nanofluids is higher than storage modulus G’ over the whole temperature scope, suggesting that dissipation of mechanical energy for the nanofluids is more effective than storge, which gives out direct proofs for liquid state feature of nanofluids. The shear stress and viscosity gradual decline along with increasing temperature (Fig. 4e and f), which can be attributed to a more flexible three-dimensional network structure at higher temperature.

The ball-on-disk friction testing machine equipped with reciprocating mode was applied to evaluate the lubrication performance of nanofluids. The steel/steel was used as the friction pair, and test time lasted for 1800 s. The nanofluids were applied directly as lubricants, and PAO-4 was used as a comparison. It can be found from Fig. 5 a that the friction coefficient of PAO-4 fluctuates greatly and is higher than nanofluids. The average friction coefficient of PAO-4 is 0.115, and average friction coefficients of all nanofluids are lower than PAO-4 (Fig. 5b). In addition, the average friction coefficients of nanofluids present the trend of first decreasing and then increasing, and the nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA shows the lowest average friction coefficient of 0.091. Compared to PAO-4, the wear volumes of nanofluids decrease by 49 % (2.5 wt% CQD/CA), 53 % (5 wt% CQD/CA), 52 % (7.5 wt% CQD/CA), 44 % (10 wt% CQD/CA), respectively (Fig. 5c).

To compare the lubrication performance differences between PAO-4 and nanofluids, the three-dimensional profilometer, optical microscope, and EDS were utilized to characterize the morphology and elemental distribution of wear scars lubricated by PAO-4 and nanofluids, and the results are displayed in the Fig. 6 . The depth of wear scar from PAO-4 is 1 μm (Fig. 6a1), and width is 336 μm (Fig. 6a3). The worn surface from PAO-4 is severely damaged, and a large number of grooves and metal adhesion can be observed on the wear scar, which indicating that the main wear mechanism is abrasive wear and adhesive wear (Fig. 6a4). The EDS analysis for worn surface manifests that only C, Fe, and O elements are detected on worn surface from PAO-4 (Fig. 6a5). Depth of wear scar from the nanofluid is 0.4 μm (Fig. 6b1), and width is 268 μm (Fig. 6b3). The surface damage of the wear scar is relatively small, and only slight grooves can be observed on worn surface (Fig. 6b4). The C, Fe, O, and N elements have been detected on worn surface from the nanofluid (Fig. 6b5). The above results indicate that compared to PAO-4, nanofluids can generate the tribofilm containing N elements at the friction interface, effectively reducing wear and protecting sliding surface. The excellent lubricity of the nanofluid origins from its three-dimensional and multiple adsorption network structure. Multiple adsorption sites can enable nanofluids to firmly adsorb at the friction interface even under the action of frictional shear forces, and the three-dimensional network structure can provide certain load-bearing capacity to hinder direct contact between friction pairs, thus reducing friction and wear. In addition, nanofluids also undergo tribochemical reactions at the sliding interface, generating the tribofilm containing N element [45–48].

To comprehensively investigate the tribological properties of the nanofluid, tribological tests were conducted under various loads and frequencies. The nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA was selected as the research object due to its best lubrication performance. The friction coefficient of the nanofluid can remain stable within the test time of 1800 s under different loads. Its average friction coefficient slightly increases, and the wear volume increases with the raise of load. The friction coefficient of the nanofluid remains stable at different frequencies, with a slight decrease in average friction coefficient and a slight increase in wear volume as the frequency increases. The reason for this phenomenon is as follows. As the frequency increases, the sliding speed increases, and the sliding distance increases within the same time, resulting in an increase in wear volume. The increase in wear volume means an increase in the frictional contact area. The increase in frictional contact area leads to a decrease in the average Hertz contact stress, resulting in a decrease in the friction coefficient. The above results indicate that load changes have little effect on the friction coefficient of nanofluids, but have a significant impact on wear. However, frequency changes have little effect on friction and wear. In addition, the nanofluid can exhibit stable friction coefficients under different loads and frequencies, indicating its potential to be applied in various working conditions.

In order to study the long-term lubrication ability of nanofluids, a two-hour friction experiment was conducted on the nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA. It can be observed from Fig. S4 that the friction coefficient remains stable during the two-hour test, with an average friction coefficient of 0.097. The wear volume is 1.31*105 μm3, and the wear scar width is 291 μm. The worn surface is smooth without obvious damage. The results indicate that nanofluids have excellent long-term lubrication ability.

The synthetic nanofluid possesses excellent water solubility and can be applied as a water-based lubricant additive. It can be observed from Fig. S5 that the nanofluid in water still exhibits good dispersion stability after being left for 30 days, which is attributed to the hydrophilic group (-COOH, -OH, and -NH2). The nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA is selected as the research object, and the water-based lubricants with different nanofluid addition amounts (0.05 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%) are prepared by dispersing the nanofluid in water. The wettability of lubricants on the substrate surface has a significant impact on their lubrication performance. Therefore, the contact angle of lubricants on the steel surface was firstly investigated, as shown in Fig. 7 a, b. It can be observed that with the increase of nanofluid addition, the contact angle of the lubricant on the steel surface gradually decreases. This indicates that the nanofluid as a water-based lubricant additive can effectively reduce the surface tension of water, improve wettability, and thus facilitate the entry of lubricants into the friction interface to exert lubricating effects. In addition, water-based lubricant additives should also have good anti-corrosion properties. Thus, the electrochemical test is conducted on 10 wt% NaCl aqueous solution added with 0.5 wt% nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) to investigate the anti-corrosion performance of nanofluids. Simultaneously, the 10 wt% NaCl aqueous solution is used as a control. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 7c, d, respectively. The corrosion current and corrosion potential (Fig. 7d) can be acquired from potentiodynamic polarization curves in Fig. 7c. The corrosion current and corrosion potential of 10 wt% NaCl solution are 3.0 × 10−5A and −0.995 V, the corrosion current and corrosion potential are 1.9 × 10−5 A and −0.951 V after the addition of 0.5 wt% nanofluid. The reduction of corrosion current and rise of corrosion potential after introduction of nanofluid indicates that the nanofluid, as a water-based lubricant additive, can also enhance the corrosion resistance of lubricants. In addition, the copper sheet is directly immersed in pure water without NaCl and pure water containing 0.5 wt% nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA), and left at 55 °C for 8 h to visually examine the anti-corrosion performance of the nanofluid. The photograph of the copper sheet after the experiment is shown in Fig. 7e. It can be observed that the copper sheet soaked in pure water has obvious corrosion marks, while the surface of the copper sheet soaked in pure water containing 0.5 wt% nanofluid shows almost no corrosion, proving that the nanofluid has anti-corrosion ability.

Lubricants are prepared by adding different concentrations of nanofluids (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) into pure water to investigate the tribological properties of nanofluids as water-based lubricant additives. The results are shown in Fig. 7f, g, and h. The friction coefficient of pure water rapidly increases to around 0.3 at the beginning of the experiment, and the friction coefficient displays significant fluctuations during the friction experiment (Fig. 7f). The addition of nanofluid can significantly reduce the friction coefficient and wear volume (Fig. 7g, h), thereinto the 0.5 wt% addition of nanofluid exhibits the best anti-wear and friction reducing performance, reducing friction by 52 % and wear by 41 %. The morphology and surface element distribution of wear scars formed by pure water and pure water containing 0.5 wt% nanofluid are further characterized, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . The depth of the wear scar corresponding to pure water is about 1.5 μm (Fig. 8a1), and the worn width is 334 μm (Fig. 8a3). The wear scar is severely damaged, and the surface is covered with peeled metal caused by adhesive wear and grooves caused by abrasive wear (Fig. 8a4). Only C, Fe and O elements are detected on the worn surface (Fig. 8a5). However, the worn depth corresponding to pure water containing 0.5 wt% nanofluid decreases to 1.2 μm (Fig. 8b1), and the width of the wear scar decreases to 273 μm (Fig. 8b3). Only grooves caused by abrasive wear are observed on the worn surface (Fig. 8b4), and C, Fe, O, N elements are found on the worn surface (Fig. 8b5).

The nanofluid possesses good water solubility, and the added nanofluid can reduce the surface tension of lubricants (Fig. 7a, b), promoting the entry of lubricants into the friction interface to exert lubricating effects. The multiple adsorption sites of the nanofluid can promote the adsorption of lubricants at the friction interface, forming a stable adsorption layer. The multiple adsorption sites of the nanofluid also enhance the interaction between lubricants and the friction interface, which is more conducive to the occurrence of interfacial tribochemical reactions and the formation of N-containing tribofilms [45]. The above reasons enable nanofluids as water-based lubricant additives to significantly enhance the lubrication effect of pure water, reduce friction and wear.

To comprehensively analyze the surface material composition of the abrasion marks, XPS was used to further analyze the surface element composition and valence states of the wear scars formed by pure water and pure water added with 0.5 wt% nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA). The results are shown in Fig. 9 . The peaks of pure water for C 1 s at 284.8 eV, 285.3 eV, 288.3 eV are attributed to C-C, C-O, C=O bonds, respectively [45,49]. The bonding energy of pure water for O 1 s at 530.2 eV, 531.4 eV, 532.3 eV correspond to Fe-O, C-O, C=O bonds, respectively. Peaks of pure water for Fe 2p at 707.3 eV, 711.0 eV, 714.1 eV, and 724.9 eV relate to Fe, FeO, Fe2O3/FeOOH, and Fe3O4. The peaks of pure water added with nanofluid for C 1 s at 284.8 eV, 285.3 eV, 288.4 eV are attributed to C-C, C-O/N, C=O bonds, respectively [50]. The bonding energy of pure water added with nanofluid for O 1 s at 530.6 eV, 531.9 eV, 532.8 eV correspond to Fe-O, C-O, C=O bonds, respectively [51,52]. Peaks of pure water added with nanofluid for Fe 2p at 707.3 eV, 711.1 eV, 713.4 eV, 725.2 eV and relate to Fe, FeO, Fe2O3/FeOOH, and Fe3O4. Peaks of pure water added with nanofluid for N 1 s at 400.1 eV are attributed to C-N bond. The XPS results are consistent with the EDS results, both indicating that the worn surface formed by pure water contains only C, Fe, and O elements, while the wear scar formed by pure water added with the nanofluid contains C, Fe, O, and N elements. This indicates that the lubricant added with the nanofluid can form the tribofilm containing N element on sliding surface, effectively reducing friction and wear. This is due to the multiple adsorption effects of -NH2, -COOH, and -CONH- in nanofluids, which can enhance the adsorption of lubricants on the substrate surface and facilitate the formation of tribofilm (Fig. 9h).

Based on the above experimental results, the lubrication mechanism of carbon quantum dots nanofluid is summarized as follows (Fig. 10 ). Firstly, relying on the reaction between the -COOH in carbon quantum dots and unreacted citric acid and the -NH2 in polyethyleneimine, different polyethyleneimine chains are interconnected, constructing a three-dimensional network structure in the nanofluid. This three-dimensional network structure can enable the nanofluid to have good load-bearing capacity and form a tough lubricating film at the friction interface. In addition, the -NH2, -COOH, and reaction formed -CONH- in the nanofluid possess good adsorption ability on the substrate surface. This multiple adsorption can effectively enhance the bonding force between the nanofluid and the substrate, thereby forming a stable lubricating film and improving the lubrication effect of the nanofluid. At the same time, strong bonding force is also conducive to the generation of N-containing tribofilms through tribochemical reactions of nanofluids during the friction process, thereby better protecting the sliding surface. Furthermore, CQDs can act as nanobearings in sliding contact, converting sliding friction into rolling friction. The three-dimensional network structure, multiple adsorption effect, and nanobearing effect bring excellent lubrication performance to nanofluids. When the nanofluid is used as the water-based lubricant additive, the three-dimensional network structure of nanofluids may not be maintained, but the multiple adsorption and nanobearing effects still exist. The strong adsorption and CQDs enable the nanofluid as the water-based lubricating additive to still exhibit excellent anti-friction and anti-wear properties.

In summary, inspired by the lubrication mechanism of snail mucus, the carbon quantum dots nanofluid is fabricated by a facile two-step synthesis. Firstly, CQDs are prepared by CA pyrolysis. Then, CQDs rich in carboxyl groups on the surface are used as crosslinking sites for polyethyleneimine, preparing high-performance nanofluid lubricants with three-dimensional and multiple adsorption networks. The nanofluids perform good fluidity under room temperature and excellent tribological behavior. The nanofluid containing 7.5 wt% CQD/CA shows a low friction coefficient of 0.091, and the wear decreases by 52 % compared to PAO-4. This three-dimensional network structure endows the nanofluid with good load-bearing capacity, forming a robust lubricating film at the friction interface. The multiple adsorption from -NH2, -COOH, and -CONH- in nanofluids can effectively enhance the bonding force between the nanofluid and substrate, forming a stable lubricating film. Furthermore, CQDs can act as nanobearings, converting sliding friction into rolling friction. Additionally, the nanofluid can also serve as the water-based lubricant additive with excellent performance, thereinto the 0.5 wt% addition of nanofluid (7.5 wt% CQD/CA) reduces friction by 52 % and wear by 41 % compared to pure water. This study has developed a novel method for preparing nanofluids, providing new design ideas for biomimetic lubricants and promoting the development of lubrication technology.

Hongxiang Yu: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Xiangyu Zuo: Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Xia Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Data curation. Xiaobo Wang: Supervision, Resources. Feng Zhou: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by the Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. XDB047020103); the Major Science and Technology Project of Gansu Province (No.23ZDGA011); the Key project of Natural Science Foundation from Gansu Province (No. 24JRRA045); the Major Basic Research Project of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2024ZD26); the Taishan Scholars Program from Shandong province (Grant No. tsqn202306318).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.161810.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transformation mechanism between the frictional interface under dioctyl sebacate lubrication

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organofluorinated Ag/2D diamond nanocomposites achieve simultaneously ultra-low friction and wear in self-lubricating epoxy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Cai, C. Jiao, J. Wu, S. Ge, W. Li, L. Chen, G. Gong, J. Yu, Y. Lv, J. Yu, K. Nishimura, N. Jiang, T. Cai, Organofluorinated Ag/2D diamond nanocomposites achieve simultaneously ultra-low friction and wear in self-lubricating epoxy. Chem. Eng. J. 496 (2024).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of polyelectrolyte-decorated polydopamine nanoparticles via surface reconstruction for enhancing oil-based lubrication

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Mou, J. Peng, R. Yan, Y. Li, B. Zhao, Z. Wang, D. Xiao, Synthesis of polyelectrolyte-decorated polydopamine nanoparticles via surface reconstruction for enhancing oil-based lubrication. Chem. Eng. J. 480 (2024).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalent-organic framework nanomaterials: energy band engineering generating ultrathin lubrication films for excellent lubrication

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, B. Zhao, R. Dong, P. Wen, M. Fan, Covalent-Organic Framework Nanomaterials: Energy Band Engineering Generating Ultrathin Lubrication Films for Excellent Lubrication. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 16 (2024) 49993-50003.
